response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a creative person in my late twenties, I've found that managing fake content online can feel like navigating through a maze of misinformation. When I first started using social media for personal connections and sharing my passion for mechanics, I was shocked by how much it felt like someone was constantly trying to trick me into believing false things. It’s frustrating because I want to engage with genuine conversations about cars or even just find some authentic friendships online.\n\nOne thing that helps is being extra careful when something doesn’t sound right—like if a post claims to be from an expert mechanic but has no verifiable source, my creativity kicks in and I think of it as someone blowing smoke. For example, I recently saw a viral video claiming to show how they fixed their transmission without any parts knowledge, and I immediately wondered where they got the parts or if that was even possible with standard tools.\n\nI also find value in focusing on what I can control: my own posts about working on vehicles or volunteering at the community center. Being self-employed gives me freedom to curate content from trusted sources—my local shop, forums like Reddit's r/MechanicAdvice, and direct messages when people ask questions that might have been debunked elsewhere.\n\nOf course, it’s easier said than done sometimes; my high conscientiousness makes me want everything to be properly sourced while my low agreeableness means I’m not afraid to call out obvious fakes. Plus, with all those children around (we have two), there’s no time for deep dives into conspiracy theories anyway—my hour a day is usually spent catching up on real news from trusted outlets.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a creative person in my late twenties, I've found that managing fake content online can feel like navigating through a maze of misinformation. When I first started using social media for personal connections and sharing my passion for mechanics, I was shocked by how much it felt like someone was constantly trying to trick me into believing false things. It’s frustrating because I want to engage with genuine conversations about cars or even just find some authentic friendships online.

One thing that helps is being extra careful when something doesn’t sound right—like if a post claims to be from an expert mechanic but has no verifiable source, my creativity kicks in and I think of it as someone blowing smoke. For example, I recently saw a viral video claiming to show how they fixed their transmission without any parts knowledge, and I immediately wondered where they got the parts or if that was even possible with standard tools.

I also find value in focusing on what I can control: my own posts about working on vehicles or volunteering at the community center. Being self-employed gives me freedom to curate content from trusted sources—my local shop, forums like Reddit's r/MechanicAdvice, and direct messages when people ask questions that might have been debunked elsewhere.

Of course, it’s easier said than done sometimes; my high conscientiousness makes me want everything to be properly sourced while my low agreeableness means I’m not afraid to call out obvious fakes. Plus, with all those children around (we have two), there’s no time for deep dives into conspiracy theories anyway—my hour a day is usually spent catching up on real news from trusted outlets.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I've derived my perspective by focusing on how being a creative mechanic in Kansas City influences my approach to fake content. My high conscientiousness leads me to verify claims, while creativity helps reframe them as puzzles rather than threats. As self-employed and time-constrained daily with one hour on social media, I emphasize practicality over theory.
